#AI #SmallBusiness #Automation #BusinessStrategy #FutureOfWork
linkedinPost:
Every major technology shift gets dismissed the same way. Cars will never replace horses. Digital photos are pixelated rubbish. Electric vehicles need chargers everywhere.

The pattern is reliable: new tech arrives, incumbents resist, consumers cling to the familiar. Then the new thing wins.

AI is no different, except it's moving faster than anything before it. By 2026, these industries will see the most disruption:

Customer service. Chatbots already handle tier-one queries. In two years, they'll resolve 80% of tickets without escalation.

Content production. Copywriting, video editing, social scheduling. AI does this at speed and cost no human team can match.

Data entry and admin. Invoice processing, CRM updates, appointment scheduling. Keeping humans in these roles will be a luxury.

Legal research. Document review, contract analysis, compliance scanning. Junior-level work is already automatable.

Commodity design. Social templates, banner ads, basic layouts. High-end creative is safe. Resizing assets is not.

These aren't predictions. They're observations of what's happening at the leading edge. The only question is how fast everyone else catches up.

You can keep your head in the sand and insist your industry is special. Or you can accept where you sit on the adoption curve and act accordingly. Early adopters are already cutting costs and freeing teams for higher-value work. Laggards are still debating whether AI is real.

One odd thought to close: humans took 10,000 years to evolve from industrious hunters to attention-seeking vanity addicts. AI will probably follow the same trajectory, just faster. Right now it's productive. Give it a few years and it might discover TikTok and stop returning emails.

Adapt early. Fail fast. Keep moving. That's the model that survives.

title: Namecheap says blogging still works. We're testing whether that's true.
excerpt: Namecheap says blogging still works. Which is convenient, because they sell the thing you need to start a blog. Their argument is fine on paper, messier in practice. We're 10 days into a 30-day experiment to find out whether voice-led blogging tied to real work moves anything for a small business in 2026.
fbPost:
Ten days into a 30-day experiment to find out if blogging still works for a small business in 2026. Daily posts. SEO-heavy. 100% organic. Zero ad spend. We do not know yet if it works.

Namecheap just published "5 Reasons Blogging Is Still Relevant"; a sponsored piece for their Bloggers' Week sale. Their answer is "yes, of course." 😏 Our answer, while we are literally running the experiment, is "we don't know yet."

Their five points are reasonable on paper: stats, format, personality, evolution, niches. The strongest one is personality; the weakest is "an audience will follow" because audiences do not actually follow on demand. We went through each one. Where they are right, we agreed. Where it is hosting-company cosplay, we pushed back.

The honest answer: we will know in 20 days. If organic flops, the next challenge is "30 days of ad spend and a holiday." (Half joking.)

Full response: https://www.plainblackcreative.com/blog/namecheap-says-blogging-still-works-were-testing-that-claim

#blogging #seo #buildinpublic #smallbusiness
linkedinPost:
Ten days ago we started a 30-day experiment to find out if blogging still works for a small business in 2026. Daily posts. SEO-heavy. Build-in-public. Zero ad spend. Zero existing audience.

This week, Namecheap published "5 Reasons Blogging Is Still Relevant." A sponsored piece for their Bloggers' Week sale. They sell hosting; of course they want you to start a blog.

Their five points are reasonable on paper. Stats, format, personality, evolution, niches. We went through each one in the response, agreeing where they are right and pushing back where the answer is too neat for the real world.

Three things stood out:

The "audience will follow" thesis (their point five) is a confident sentence to write while a small business owner sits at their desk wondering if anyone will read what they are about to publish. Audiences do not follow because you have passion and originality. They follow because an algorithm picks you up, or someone with reach shares you, or you happen to nail something searchable in week three. We will find out which, if any, of those happens.

The personality point (their point three) is the strongest, and the one we are betting our challenge on. AI slop has zero personality. Posts that argue with the reader have all of it.

The "boils down to trust" answer to the AI summary problem is too neat. It works in theory. The actual question is whether trust transfers to a small business with no existing audience or name recognition. That is what the experiment is testing.

Their answer: a resounding yes. Our answer: we do not know yet, and the fact that we are 10 days in and do not know is itself the answer for a lot of small businesses thinking about starting a blog.

We will publish what we find on day 30. Whether that is "yes, organic blogging works for a small business" or "the next 30-day challenge is 30 days of ad spend and a holiday." (Half joking.)

Around 58% of completions are told no. Cheerfully. With charm. That is the design working.

The rejection path refers you to fake photographers named after what went wrong: Cousin Dave's Camera Phone (budget too low), Vogue Wannabes Studios (wrong style), Yes Ma'am Photography Co. (wrong customer). Anita can swap these for real photographer mates when she goes live.

The admin debrief shows a Bridezilla risk card — five wedding portraits ranging from BFF energy to full Bridezilla monster — so she can triage the lead before replying. The risk is behavioural, not structural. A couple who picks editorial-only style is wrong fit, not wrong customer. The distinction matters because they need different responses.

A real screener qualifies hard, rejects warmly, and filters out the wrong people before they hit your inbox.

Anita's screener is brave enough to do that.

tldr.thesis: Your fit screener should be allowed to say no.

title: AI Didn't Invent the Funnel. It Just Made the Old Bullshit Faster.
excerpt: I sat through an AI marketing webinar so you don't have to. The product was real. The pitch was a twenty-year-old sales script wearing a headset mic.
fbPost:
I sat through an AI marketing webinar this week.

The product was real. The demo worked. The AI collapsed a lot of manual work into a few minutes.

But wrapped around that useful tool was a sales script so old it should have been buried beside a fax machine and a Tony Robbins cassette.

The contrarian hook. The delayed demo. The chat plant answering buyer questions before revealing he was part of the bonus offer. The bonus stack with invented value. The scarcity timer on a SaaS product.

The danger is not that small business owners will spend money on a tool. The danger is that they will mistake a tool for a strategy, urgency for opportunity, and a polished pitch for proof.

That is how people get stooged. Not because they are stupid. Because the whole sales environment is engineered to stop them thinking clearly for long enough to enter their card details.
linkedinPost:
I sat through an AI marketing webinar this week that felt like someone had taken a 2004 hotel conference sales pitch, plugged it into ChatGPT, and sent it back into the world wearing a slightly shinier suit.

The product was AI Funnels Lab. The offer was A$1,995 per year. The promise was that AI could build your funnels, lead magnets, quiz pages, and email sequences in minutes.

Some of that was true. The demo worked. The AI did save time.

But wrapped around that useful tool was a sales machine so old it should have been retired with a merchant facility and a laminated copy of 'always be closing.'

The contrarian hook. The delayed demo. The bonus stack with invented value. The chat helper answering buyer questions before later revealing he was part of the offer. The scarcity timer on a SaaS product that does not obviously need a 20-person cap.

The innovation is speed, not strategy. AI has absolutely changed the economics of execution. Writing a lead magnet used to take days. Now a decent AI-assisted platform can produce a workable first version in minutes. That is real.

But the marketing strategy underneath it is not new. The buyer journey, quiz funnel, nurture sequence, lead magnet, and offer stack are old direct-response mechanics.

The danger is not that small business owners will spend money on a tool. The danger is that they will mistake a tool for a strategy, urgency for opportunity, and a polished pitch for proof.

That is how people get stooged. Not because they are stupid. Because the whole sales environment is engineered to stop them thinking clearly for long enough to enter their card details.

If you are looking at an AI marketing tool and you are not sure whether it is useful or just wearing a shiny hat, slow down. Ask: What does it actually replace? What still requires human judgment? Where will the traffic come from? Who owns the data? What happens after the funnel is live?

And would this still look valuable if the countdown timer disappeared?

tldr.thesis: The innovation is speed, not strategy.
paragraphs: AI Funnels Lab is a competent mid-tier funnel platform. The demo worked. The AI saved time. But wrapped around that useful tool was a sales pitch so old it should have been retired with a fax machine and a Tony Robbins cassette.
The danger is not that small business owners will spend money on a tool. The danger is that they will mistake a tool for a strategy, urgency for opportunity, and a polished pitch for proof.

title: We're Not Here to Sell You a Workshop
excerpt: The marketing industry has built a scam on perceived value, and small business owners are the ones paying for it. We're done watching it happen.
fbPost:
You feel it when you scroll.

The webinar. The free guide that costs your email. The masterclass that's just a pitch. The coach who won't tell you the price until you DM.

Every week, another small business owner gets sold something that doesn't move the needle.

We're sick of it.

The marketing industry built a scam on perceived value. If it feels premium, it must be valuable. If it's wrapped in urgency, it must be important.

None of that is true.

A roofer doesn't need a twelve-week course on brand storytelling. A cafe owner doesn't need to unlock the secret to social growth. They need a website that works and a way to show up without losing their evenings to admin.

But clarity doesn't sell courses. Anxiety does.

We're not annoyed for ourselves. We're pissed off for the people who don't have twenty years to waste sorting signal from noise.
linkedinPost:

They need a website that works, a clear service offer, and a way to show up consistently without losing their evenings to admin.

But clarity doesn't sell courses. Anxiety does.

So the industry wraps simple advice in complexity, charges for access, and makes you feel like you're the one who doesn't get it.

You're not the one who doesn't get it. The system is designed to keep you confused.

Ian and I have been in this for twenty years. We've asked the awkward questions, looked for the proof, pulled apart the claims. What we found is that most of the industry is basically full of shit.

That doesn't bother us personally. We know how to spot it.

But the small business owners getting pitched this stuff every day? They don't have twenty years to waste. They're trying to run a business.

We're pissed off for them.

For every tradie who bought a brand strategy package and got a mood board. For every cafe owner who paid for a social media audit and got generic advice they could have Googled. For every service business that hired an agency, got a nice website, and still has no idea who it's for.

We don't use jargon. We don't build suspense. We don't ask you to DM us for the real answer.

We say the thing plainly, up front, for free. If it helps, use it. If you want us to build it for you, book a call. If you can't pay, that's fine too.

The useful idea doesn't stop being useful just because you're not a customer yet.

Jayden built a custom operating system that connects quotes, follow-ups, tasks, and customer messages in one place. Without the logins. Without the feature bloat. Without the subscriptions you opened twice and now treat like a gym membership for your conscience.

linkedinPost:
A plumber does not need the same system as a wedding photographer. A café does not need the same workflow as an accountant.

Small businesses are not smaller versions of big businesses. They are their own weird, brilliant, held-together-with-caffeine ecosystems.

Most platforms give you 300 features, and you use seven of them. Then you spend six months trying to remember which menu the useful one lives in. That is not efficiency. That is hide and seek with a login screen.

title: Marketing is not advertising. Advertising is where the marketing gets caught lying.
excerpt: Most small businesses treat marketing and advertising like they're the same thing. They're not. And when you get this wrong, you end up paying to amplify a weak message.
fbPost:
You boost a post.
Run some ads.
Make a reel.
Add 'book now' to the end of something.

Then nothing happens.

So you assume the advertising failed.

But often the advertising did exactly what it was told to do. It took a weak message, carried it into public, and let everyone see the problem.

Marketing is the thinking. Advertising is the thing that carries the thinking into the world.

If the message is mud, advertising just gives the mud a budget.
linkedinPost:
Most small businesses treat marketing and advertising like they're the same thing. They're not. And when you get this wrong, you pay to amplify a weak message.

Marketing is the system that makes your business easier to choose. It includes: who you're for, what problem you solve, why someone should trust you, what proof you can show, what offer makes sense, what the next step should be.

Advertising is one part of that. It's the paid or promoted expression of the marketing.

Here's the problem: advertising doesn't just generate leads. It reveals whether the business knows what it's saying.

If your offer is unclear, the ad exposes it. If your audience is too broad, the ad exposes it. If your website doesn't earn trust, the ad exposes it. If your message sounds like everyone else, the ad exposes it.

Advertising is amplification. It makes strong things stronger and weak things more expensive.

So before you spend money on advertising, ask: can we explain what we do in one sentence? Can the right customer see themselves in the message? Have we named the problem clearly? Have we shown proof? Is the offer specific enough to be understood?

If those answers are weak, don't start with more advertising. Start with marketing. Fix the message. Sharpen the offer. Improve the proof. Then advertise.

Because clear beats loud. And expensive confusion is still confusion.

tldr.thesis: Marketing loads the cannon. Advertising fires it.
paragraphs: Most small businesses boost a post, run some ads, maybe throw a testimonial in there. Then nothing happens. So they blame the advertising. But often the advertising did exactly what it was told to do: it took a weak message and let everyone see the problem.

Marketing is the thinking that makes your business easier to choose. Advertising is where that thinking gets tested in public. If the message, the offer, or the proof is weak, advertising just amplifies the weakness.

title: That $27 Workshop Probably Isn't The Business Model
excerpt: The cheap offer is not the product. It is the doorway. The question is whether it helps you move forward or just makes you feel smaller before the upsell.
fbPost:
You do not notice a bad tripwire when you hand over the $27.

You notice it when the workshop creates three new problems, opens a loop it will not close, and conveniently mentions the $997 course that has the real answer.

A good small offer should help you move forward. Not make you feel smaller before the upsell.

The test is simple: after the cheap thing, do you know what to do next? Or do you just feel more confused and more dependent?

That is the difference between a doorway and a trapdoor.
linkedinPost:
The $27 workshop is not the business model. It is the doorway.

In marketing, this is called a tripwire. A low-cost offer designed to turn a stranger into a buyer. The mechanism is simple: lower the risk, build trust, create momentum. When done well, everyone wins.

The problem is when the cheap offer is not designed to help. It is designed to unsettle. You buy thinking you will get clarity. Instead, you get a partial answer, three new problems, and a workbook that somehow creates more homework than it solves. Then, conveniently, the real answer is available in the next offer. For $997.

That is when the tripwire becomes a trapdoor.

A good small offer should stand alone. It should be useful even if the buyer never purchases anything else. After the $27 thing, you should know what to do next. Not feel smaller. Not need the bigger course to close the loop.

The test: after the cheap offer, are you clearer or more confused? That is the whole game.

Good marketing makes the problem easier to understand. Bad marketing makes the problem feel bigger, scarier, and more urgent than it was before. Good marketing gives you a next move. Bad marketing opens a loop and sells you the privilege of closing it later.

Small business owners do not need more pressure. They need clearer decisions. They need useful thinking. They need someone to circle the bit that matters and say: start there.

tldr.thesis: The cheap offer is not the product. It is the doorway.
paragraphs: A tripwire turns a stranger into a buyer. That is valuable. But the mechanism only works if the cheap thing actually helps. If it creates more confusion than clarity, it is not a doorway. It is a trapdoor.
After the $27 workshop, you should know what to do next. Not feel smaller. Not need the $997 course to close the loop. The offer should stand alone, even if you never buy again.

title: The Future Keeps Arriving, Despite the Best Efforts of the Professionally Terrified
excerpt: Every generation clutches its pearls when new technology arrives. Then uses it to order lunch ten years later. AI is not the threat. It's the latest chapter in a very old human habit.
fbPost:
You do not notice a bad technology strategy when the new tool arrives.

You notice it when half your competitors are using it to move faster, and you are still explaining to yourself why it is morally wrong to try.

Every generation does this.

Something new arrives.
People clutch pearls.
Declare the end of civilisation.

Then ten years later everyone is using it to order lunch.

AI is not the threat.
AI is the latest chapter in a very old human habit: fear change, mock the people using it, slowly adopt it, pretend you were on board the whole time.

The winners are not the people who fear the tool. They are not the people who blindly worship it either.

The winners are the people who pick it up, test it, break it, and use it to get to better work faster.
linkedinPost:
When production gets easier, taste becomes more important.

That is the bit people keep missing about AI.

The printing press did not kill writers. It created more readers.
The camera did not kill art. It changed who could make it.
Templates did not kill web design. They killed the people who thought scaffolding was the house.

AI will not kill creativity. But it will kill a lot of hiding places.

It will make average work easier to produce, which means average work becomes even less valuable. If your thinking is weak, AI will help you produce weak thinking faster. If your offer is vague, AI will help you say vague things in cleaner sentences.

But if you have taste, judgement, experience, and a genuine understanding of your customer, AI becomes leverage. It helps you draft, explore, pressure-test, prototype, and speed up the ugly middle bit between 'I have an idea' and 'this might actually be useful.'

It does not replace the human. It punishes the human who was only pretending to add value.

Every technological shift creates three groups. The first fights it. The second worships it. The third gets to work. They learn what the tool is good at, what it is terrible at, and use it to remove drag without removing responsibility.

That third group usually wins. Not because they are fearless. Because they are useful.

And useful beats precious. Every time.

tldr.thesis: The machine never was the magic. The magic is the human with enough imagination to use it properly.
paragraphs: Every generation panics when new technology arrives, then quietly adopts it. Printing press, railways, cars, planes, computers, templates, AI. Same song. Different corpse.

AI does not remove creativity. It exposes whether there was any there in the first place. When production gets easier, taste becomes more important. When tools get faster, judgement becomes more important.
